# Isabelle's COMP110 Data Analysis: Student Learning Insights

## Data Analysis Overview

This project analyzes COMP110 student survey data to understand patterns in learning, confidence, and engagement, suggesting ways the course can improve, including the introduction of more low-stakes mini quizzes. The results are visualized using Python and Seaborn.

## Key Findings

- Students generally reported moderate to high understanding of course material.
- Value levels vary more widely than perceived understanding.
- Most students found assignments somewhat valuable for learning.

## Visualization
The data was analyzed using Seaborn plots to show relationships between quiz effectiveness, understanding, and value. 

<img src="{{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/output.png" width="600"/>

<img src="{{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/output2.png" width="600"/>

<img src="{{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/output3.png" width="600"/>

## Conclusion

The analysis suggests that students who rated quizzes as more effective also tend to report higher levels of understanding and value in the course material. The scatterplot shows a positive relationship between quiz effectiveness and understanding, indicating that frequent low-stakes quizzes may reinforce learning.

The distribution of responses also shows that most students rated quizzes as moderately to highly effective, which supports the idea that quizzes are a valuable learning tool.

Based on these findings, increasing the number of short, low-stakes quizzes would likely improve student learning outcomes. However, there may be trade-offs such as increased workload for students and additional grading or preparation time for instructors.
